## Contents

# History of climate change science

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Tyndalls_setup_for_measuring_radiant_heat_absorption_by_gases_annotated.svg/500px-Tyndalls_setup_for_measuring_radiant_heat_absorption_by_gases_annotated.svg.png)

The **history of the scientific discovery of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change")** began in the early 19th century when [ice ages](/wiki/Ice_ages "Ice ages") and other natural changes in [paleoclimate](/wiki/Paleoclimate "Paleoclimate") were first suspected and the natural [greenhouse effect](/wiki/Greenhouse_effect "Greenhouse effect") was first identified. In the late 19th century, scientists first argued that human emissions of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gases "Greenhouse gases") could change [Earth's energy balance](/wiki/Earth%27s_energy_balance "Earth's energy balance") and [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate"). The existence of the greenhouse effect, while not named as such, was proposed as early as 1824 by [Joseph Fourier](/wiki/Joseph_Fourier "Joseph Fourier").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The argument and the evidence were further strengthened by [Claude Pouillet](/wiki/Claude_Pouillet "Claude Pouillet") in 1827 and 1838. In 1856 [Eunice Newton Foote](/wiki/Eunice_Newton_Foote "Eunice Newton Foote") demonstrated that the warming effect of the sun is greater for air with water vapour than for dry air, and the effect is even greater with carbon dioxide.[[3]](#cite_note-FooteCircumstances-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[John Tyndall](/wiki/John_Tyndall "John Tyndall") was the first to measure the infrared absorption and emission of various gases and vapors. From 1859 onwards, he showed that the effect was due to a very small proportion of the atmosphere, with the main gases having no effect, and was largely due to water vapor, though small percentages of hydrocarbons and carbon dioxide had a significant effect.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The effect was more fully quantified by [Svante Arrhenius](/wiki/Svante_Arrhenius "Svante Arrhenius") in 1896, who made the first quantitative prediction of global warming due to a hypothetical doubling of atmospheric carbon dioxide.

In the 1960s, the evidence for the warming effect of [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") gas became increasingly convincing. Scientists also discovered that human activities that generated atmospheric [aerosols](/wiki/Aerosol "Aerosol") (e.g., "[air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution")") could have cooling effects as well (later referred to as [global dimming](/wiki/Global_dimming "Global dimming")). Other theories for the causes of global warming were also proposed, involving forces from [volcanism](/wiki/Volcanism "Volcanism") to [solar variation](/wiki/Solar_variation "Solar variation"). During the 1970s, scientific understanding of global warming greatly increased.

By the 1990s, as the result of improving the accuracy of [computer models](/wiki/Computer_models "Computer models") and observational work confirming the [Milankovitch theory](/wiki/Milankovitch_theory "Milankovitch theory") of the ice ages, a consensus position formed. It became clear that greenhouse gases were deeply involved in most climate changes and human-caused emissions were bringing discernible [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming").

Since the 1990s, scientific research on climate change has included multiple disciplines and has expanded. Research has expanded the understanding of causal relations, links with historic data, and abilities to measure and model climate change. Research during this period has been summarized in the Assessment Reports by the [Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change](/wiki/Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change "Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change"), with the [First Assessment Report](/wiki/IPCC_First_Assessment_Report "IPCC First Assessment Report") coming out in 1990.

## Prior to the 20th century

### Regional changes, antiquity through 19th century

From ancient times, people suspected that the climate of a region could change over the course of centuries. For example, [Theophrastus](/wiki/Theophrastus "Theophrastus"), a pupil of [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") [philosopher](/wiki/Greek_philosophy "Greek philosophy") [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") in the 4th century BC, told how the draining of marshes had made a particular locality more susceptible to freezing, and speculated that lands became warmer when the clearing of forests exposed them to sunlight. In the 1st century BC, [Roman writer](/wiki/Latin_literature "Latin literature") and [architect](/wiki/Roman_architecture "Roman architecture") [Vitruvius](/wiki/Vitruvius "Vitruvius") wrote about climate in relation to housing architecture and how to choose locations for cities.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Renaissance European](/wiki/Renaissance_Europe "Renaissance Europe") and later scholars saw that [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation"), and [grazing](/wiki/Grazing "Grazing") had altered the lands around [the Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Basin "Mediterranean Basin") since ancient times; they thought it plausible that these human interventions had affected the local weather.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) [In his book](/wiki/Dream_Pool_Essays "Dream Pool Essays") published in 1088, [Northern Song dynasty](/wiki/Northern_Song_dynasty "Northern Song dynasty") Chinese scholar and statesman [Shen Kuo](/wiki/Shen_Kuo "Shen Kuo") promoted the theory of gradual climate change over centuries of time once ancient [petrified](/wiki/Petrified "Petrified") [bamboos](/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo") were found to be preserved underground in the dry climate zone and arid northern region of *Yanzhou*, now modern day [Yan'an](/wiki/Yan%27an "Yan'an"), [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi") province, far from the warmer, wetter [climate areas of China](/wiki/Environment_of_China "Environment of China") where bamboos typically grow.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-nj-11)

The 18th and 19th-century conversion of Eastern North America from forest to [croplands](/wiki/Farm "Farm") brought obvious change within a human lifetime. From the early 19th century, many believed the transformation was altering the region's climate—probably for the better. When farmers in America, dubbed "sodbusters", took over the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains"), they held that "[rain follows the plow](/wiki/Rain_follows_the_plow "Rain follows the plow")".[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Collins_Sodbuster-13) Other experts disagreed, and some argued that deforestation caused rapid rainwater run-off and flooding, and could even result in reduced rainfall. European academics, suggesting that the temperate zones inhabited by the "[Caucasian race](/wiki/Caucasian_race "Caucasian race")" were naturally superior for the spread of civilization, proffered that the Orientals of the Ancient Near East had heedlessly converted their once lush lands into impoverished deserts.[[14]](#cite_note-aiphist2-14)

Meanwhile, national weather agencies had begun to compile masses of reliable observations of temperature, rainfall, and the like. When these figures were analyzed, they showed many rises and dips, but no steady long-term change. By the end of the 19th century, scientific opinion had turned decisively against any belief in a human influence on climate. And whatever the regional effects, few imagined that humans could affect the climate of the planet as a whole.[[14]](#cite_note-aiphist2-14)

### Paleo-climate change and theories of its causes, 19th century

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Siebenschneiderstein_1.jpg/250px-Siebenschneiderstein_1.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Fourier2_-_restoration1.jpg/250px-Fourier2_-_restoration1.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/James_Croll.jpg/250px-James_Croll.jpg)

From the mid-17th century, naturalists attempted to reconcile [mechanical philosophy](/wiki/Mechanical_philosophy "Mechanical philosophy") with theology, initially within a [biblical timescale](/wiki/Ussher_chronology "Ussher chronology"). By the late 18th century, there was increasing acceptance of prehistoric epochs. Geologists found evidence of a succession of [geological ages](/wiki/Geologic_time_scale "Geologic time scale") with climate changes. There were various competing theories about these changes; [Buffon](/wiki/Georges-Louis_Leclerc,_Comte_de_Buffon "Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon") proposed that the Earth had begun as an incandescent globe and was very gradually cooling. [James Hutton](/wiki/James_Hutton "James Hutton"), whose ideas of cyclic change over huge periods were later dubbed [uniformitarianism](/wiki/Uniformitarianism "Uniformitarianism"), was among those who found signs of past glacial activity in places too warm for [glaciers](/wiki/Glaciers "Glaciers") in modern times.[[15]](#cite_note-Davis-15)

In 1815 [Jean-Pierre Perraudin](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Perraudin "Jean-Pierre Perraudin") described for the first time how glaciers might be responsible for the giant boulders seen in alpine valleys. As he hiked in the [Val de Bagnes](/wiki/Val_de_Bagnes "Val de Bagnes"), he noticed giant granite rocks that were scattered around the narrow valley. He knew that it would take an exceptional force to move such large rocks. He also noticed how glaciers left stripes on the land and concluded that it was the ice that had carried the boulders down into the valleys.[[16]](#cite_note-nasa-paleo-16)

His idea was initially met with disbelief. [Jean de Charpentier](/wiki/Jean_de_Charpentier "Jean de Charpentier") wrote, "I found his hypothesis so extraordinary and even so extravagant that I considered it as not worth examining or even considering."[[17]](#cite_note-Imbrie-17) Despite Charpentier's initial rejection, Perraudin eventually convinced [Ignaz Venetz](/wiki/Ignaz_Venetz "Ignaz Venetz") that it might be worth studying. Venetz convinced Charpentier, who in turn convinced the influential scientist [Louis Agassiz](/wiki/Louis_Agassiz "Louis Agassiz") that the glacial theory had merit.[[16]](#cite_note-nasa-paleo-16)

Agassiz developed a theory of what he termed "[Ice Age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age")"—when glaciers covered Europe and much of North America. In 1837 Agassiz was the first to scientifically propose that the Earth had been subject to a past [ice age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age").[[18]](#cite_note-Evans_1887-18) [William Buckland](/wiki/William_Buckland "William Buckland") had been a leading proponent in Britain of [flood geology](/wiki/Flood_geology "Flood geology"), later dubbed [catastrophism](/wiki/Catastrophism "Catastrophism"), which accounted for erratic boulders and other "diluvium" as relics of the [Biblical flood](/wiki/Deluge_myth "Deluge myth"). This was strongly opposed by [Charles Lyell](/wiki/Charles_Lyell "Charles Lyell")'s version of Hutton's uniformitarianism and was gradually abandoned by Buckland and other catastrophist geologists. A field trip to the Alps with Agassiz in October 1838 convinced Buckland that features in Britain had been caused by glaciation, and both he and Lyell strongly supported the ice age theory which became widely accepted by the 1870s.[[15]](#cite_note-Davis-15)

Before the concept of ice ages was proposed, [Joseph Fourier](/wiki/Joseph_Fourier "Joseph Fourier") in 1824 reasoned based on physics that Earth's atmosphere kept the planet warmer than would be the case in a vacuum. Fourier recognized that the atmosphere transmitted [visible light](/wiki/Visible_light "Visible light") waves efficiently to the earth's surface. The earth then absorbed visible light and emitted [infrared radiation](/wiki/Infrared_radiation "Infrared radiation") in response, but the atmosphere did not transmit infrared efficiently, which therefore increased surface temperatures. He also suspected that human activities could influence the radiation balance and Earth's climate, although he focused primarily on [land-use changes](/wiki/Land-use_change "Land-use change"). In an 1827 paper, Fourier stated,[[19]](#cite_note-fourier1-19)

The establishment and progress of human societies, the action of natural forces, can notably change, and in vast regions, the state of the surface, the distribution of water and the great movements of the air. Such effects are able to make to vary, in the course of many centuries, the average degree of heat; because the analytic expressions contain coefficients relating to the state of the surface and which greatly influence the temperature.

Fourier's work built on previous discoveries: in 1681 [Edme Mariotte](/wiki/Edme_Mariotte "Edme Mariotte") noted that glass, though transparent to sunlight, obstructs [radiant heat](/wiki/Radiant_heat "Radiant heat").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Around 1774 [Horace Bénédict de Saussure](/wiki/Horace_B%C3%A9n%C3%A9dict_de_Saussure "Horace Bénédict de Saussure") showed that non-luminous warm objects emit [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") heat, and used a glass-topped insulated box to trap and measure heat from sunlight.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The physicist [Claude Pouillet](/wiki/Claude_Pouillet "Claude Pouillet") proposed in 1838 that water vapor and carbon dioxide might trap infrared and warm the atmosphere, but there was still no experimental evidence of these gases absorbing heat from thermal radiation.[[24]](#cite_note-Science_Hist_1st_believer-24)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/18560823_Eunice_Newton_Foote_-_greenhouse_effect.png/250px-18560823_Eunice_Newton_Foote_-_greenhouse_effect.png)

The warming effect of sunlight on different gases was examined in 1856 by [Eunice Newton Foote](/wiki/Eunice_Newton_Foote "Eunice Newton Foote"), who described her experiments using glass tubes exposed to sunlight. The warming effect of the sun was greater for compressed air than for an evacuated tube and greater for moist air than dry air. "Thirdly, the highest effect of the sun's rays I have found to be in carbonic acid gas." (carbon dioxide) She continued: "An atmosphere of that gas would give to our earth a high temperature; and if, as some suppose, at one period of its history, the air had mixed with it a larger proportion than at present, an increased temperature from its action, as well as from an increased weight, must have necessarily resulted." Her work was presented by Prof. [Joseph Henry](/wiki/Joseph_Henry "Joseph Henry") at the [American Association for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/American_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "American Association for the Advancement of Science") meeting in August 1856 and described as a brief note written by then journalist [David Ames Wells](/wiki/David_Ames_Wells "David Ames Wells"); her paper was published later that year in the *[American Journal of Science and Arts](/wiki/American_Journal_of_Science "American Journal of Science")*. Few noticed the paper and it was only rediscovered in the 21st century,[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[3]](#cite_note-FooteCircumstances-3)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[John Tyndall](/wiki/John_Tyndall "John Tyndall") took Fourier's work one step further in 1859 when he built an apparatus to investigate the absorption of infrared radiation in different gases. He found that water vapor, [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") like [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane") (CH4), and [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") (CO2) strongly block the radiation. He understood that without these gases the planet would rapidly freeze.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Some scientists suggested that ice ages and other great climate changes were due to changes in the amount of gases emitted in [volcanism](/wiki/Volcanism "Volcanism"). But that was only one of many possible causes. Another obvious possibility was [solar variation](/wiki/Solar_variation "Solar variation"). Shifts in [ocean currents](/wiki/Ocean_current "Ocean current") also might explain many climate changes. For changes over millions of years, the raising and lowering of mountain ranges would change patterns of both winds and ocean currents. Or perhaps the climate of a continent had not changed at all, but it had grown warmer or cooler because of [polar wander](/wiki/True_polar_wander "True polar wander") (the North Pole shifting to where the Equator had been or the like). There were dozens of theories.

For example, in the mid-19th century, [James Croll](/wiki/James_Croll "James Croll") published calculations of how the gravitational pulls of the Sun, Moon, and planets subtly affect the Earth's motion and orientation. The inclination of the Earth's axis and the shape of its orbit around the Sun oscillate gently in cycles lasting tens of thousands of years. During some periods the Northern Hemisphere would get slightly less sunlight during the winter than it would get during other centuries. Snow would accumulate, reflecting sunlight and leading to a self-sustaining ice age.[[17]](#cite_note-Imbrie-17)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Most scientists, however, found Croll's ideas—and every other theory of climate change—unconvincing.

### First calculations of greenhouse effect, 1896

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Arrhenius2.jpg/250px-Arrhenius2.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/T.C.Chamberlin.gif/250px-T.C.Chamberlin.gif)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/19021015_Hint_to_Coal_Consumers_-_Svante_Arrhenius_-_The_Selma_Morning_Times_-_Global_warming.jpg/250px-19021015_Hint_to_Coal_Consumers_-_Svante_Arrhenius_-_The_Selma_Morning_Times_-_Global_warming.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/19120814_Coal_Consumption_Affecting_Climate_-_Rodney_and_Otamatea_Times.jpg/250px-19120814_Coal_Consumption_Affecting_Climate_-_Rodney_and_Otamatea_Times.jpg)

By the late 1890s, [Samuel Pierpoint Langley](/wiki/Samuel_Pierpoint_Langley "Samuel Pierpoint Langley") along with [Frank W. Very](/wiki/Frank_W._Very "Frank W. Very")[[33]](#cite_note-33) had attempted to determine the surface temperature of the Moon by measuring infrared radiation leaving the Moon and reaching the Earth.[[34]](#cite_note-longthaw-34) The angle of the Moon in the sky when a scientist took a measurement determined how much CO2 and water vapor the Moon's radiation had to pass through to reach the Earth's surface, resulting in weaker measurements when the Moon was low in the sky. This result was unsurprising given that scientists had known about [infrared radiation absorption](/wiki/Absorption_spectroscopy "Absorption spectroscopy") for decades.

In 1896 [Svante Arrhenius](/wiki/Svante_Arrhenius "Svante Arrhenius") used Langley's observations of increased infrared absorption where Moon rays pass through the atmosphere at a low angle, encountering more [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") (CO2), to estimate an atmospheric cooling effect from a future decrease of CO2. He realized that the cooler atmosphere would hold less water vapor (another [greenhouse gas](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas")) and calculated the additional cooling effect. He also realized the cooling would increase snow and ice cover at high latitudes, making the planet reflect more sunlight and thus further cool down, as [James Croll](/wiki/James_Croll "James Croll") had hypothesized. Overall Arrhenius calculated that cutting CO2 in half would suffice to produce an ice age. He further calculated that a doubling of atmospheric CO2 would give a total warming of 5–6 degrees Celsius.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Further, Arrhenius' colleague [Arvid Högbom](/wiki/Arvid_H%C3%B6gbom "Arvid Högbom"), who was quoted in length in Arrhenius' 1896 study *On the Influence of Carbonic Acid in the Air upon the Temperature of the Earth*[[36]](#cite_note-36) had been attempting to quantify natural sources of emissions of CO2 for purposes of understanding the global [carbon cycle](/wiki/Carbon_cycle "Carbon cycle"). Högbom found that estimated carbon production from industrial sources in the 1890s (mainly coal burning) was comparable with the natural sources.[[37]](#cite_note-aiphistory-37)
Arrhenius saw that this human emission of carbon would eventually lead to a warming [energy imbalance](/wiki/Earth%27s_Energy_Imbalance "Earth's Energy Imbalance"). However, because of the relatively low rate of CO2 production in 1896, Arrhenius thought the warming would take thousands of years, and he expected it would be beneficial to humanity.[[37]](#cite_note-aiphistory-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) In 1908 he revised this prediction to take hundreds of years due to the ever increasing rate of fuel use and that within his lifetime this would benefit humanity. [[39]](#cite_note-39)

In 1899 [Thomas Chrowder Chamberlin](/wiki/Thomas_Chrowder_Chamberlin "Thomas Chrowder Chamberlin") developed at length the idea that climate changes could result from changes in the concentration of atmospheric carbon dioxide.[[40]](#cite_note-WH1965-40) Chamberlin wrote in his 1899 book, *An Attempt to Frame a Working Hypothesis of the Cause of Glacial Periods on an Atmospheric Basis*:

By the investigations of Tyndall, Lecher and Pretner, Keller, Roentgen, and Arrhenius, it has been shown that the carbon dioxide and water vapor of the atmosphere have remarkable power of absorbing and temporarily retaining heat rays, while the oxygen, nitrogen, and argon of the atmosphere possess this power in a feeble degree only. It follows that the effect of the carbon dioxide and water vapor is to blanket the earth with a thermally absorbent envelope. .. The general results assignable to a greatly increased or a greatly reduced quantity of atmospheric carbon dioxide and water may be summarized as follows:

The term "[greenhouse effect](/wiki/Greenhouse_effect "Greenhouse effect")" for this warming was introduced by [Nils Gustaf Ekholm](/wiki/Nils_Ekholm "Nils Ekholm") in 1901.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## 20th century onwards

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/191203_Furnaces_of_the_world_-_Popular_Mechanics_-_Global_warming.jpg/250px-191203_Furnaces_of_the_world_-_Popular_Mechanics_-_Global_warming.jpg)

### Paleoclimates and sunspots, early 1900s to 1950s

Arrhenius's calculations were disputed and subsumed into a larger debate over whether atmospheric changes had caused the ice ages. Experimental attempts to measure infrared absorption in the laboratory seemed to show little differences resulted from increasing CO2 levels, and also found significant overlap between absorption by CO2 and absorption by water vapor, all of which suggested that increasing carbon dioxide emissions would have little climatic effect. These early experiments were later found to be insufficiently accurate, given the instrumentation of the time. Many scientists also thought that the oceans would quickly absorb any excess carbon dioxide.[[37]](#cite_note-aiphistory-37)

Other theories of the causes of climate change fared no better. The principal advances were in observational [paleoclimatology](/wiki/Paleoclimatology "Paleoclimatology"), as scientists in various fields of [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology") worked out methods to reveal ancient climates. In 1929, [Wilmot H. Bradley](/wiki/Wilmot_H._Bradley "Wilmot H. Bradley") found that annual [varves](/wiki/Varve "Varve") of clay laid down in lake beds showed climate cycles. [Andrew Ellicott Douglass](/wiki/A._E._Douglass "A. E. Douglass") saw strong indications of climate change in [tree rings](/wiki/Tree_rings "Tree rings"). Noting that the rings were thinner in dry years, he reported climate effects from solar variations, particularly in connection with the 17th-century dearth of [sunspots](/wiki/Sunspots "Sunspots") (the [Maunder Minimum](/wiki/Maunder_Minimum "Maunder Minimum")) noticed previously by [William Herschel](/wiki/William_Herschel "William Herschel") and others. Other scientists, however, found good reason to doubt that tree rings could reveal anything beyond random regional variations. The value of tree rings for climate study was not solidly established until the 1960s.[[44]](#cite_note-aipSun-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Hufbauer-45)

Through the 1930s the most persistent advocate of a solar-climate connection was astrophysicist [Charles Greeley Abbot](/wiki/Charles_Greeley_Abbot "Charles Greeley Abbot"). By the early 1920s, he had concluded that the [solar "constant"](/wiki/Solar_constant "Solar constant") was misnamed: his observations showed large variations, which he connected with [sunspots](/wiki/Sunspots "Sunspots") passing across the face of the Sun. He and a few others pursued the topic into the 1960s, convinced that sunspot variations were a main cause of climate change. Other scientists were skeptical.[[44]](#cite_note-aipSun-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Hufbauer-45) Nevertheless, attempts to connect the [solar cycle](/wiki/Solar_cycle "Solar cycle") with climate cycles were popular in the 1920s and 1930s. Respected scientists announced correlations that they insisted were reliable enough to make predictions. Sooner or later, every prediction failed, and the subject fell into disrepute.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Milutin_Milankovi%C4%87_2.jpg/250px-Milutin_Milankovi%C4%87_2.jpg)

Meanwhile, [Milutin Milankovitch](/wiki/Milutin_Milankovitch "Milutin Milankovitch"), building on [James Croll](/wiki/James_Croll "James Croll")'s theory, improved the tedious calculations of the varying distances and angles of the Sun's radiation as the Sun and Moon gradually perturbed the Earth's orbit. Some observations of [varves](/wiki/Varves "Varves") (layers seen in the mud covering the bottom of lakes) matched the prediction of a [Milankovitch cycle](/wiki/Milankovitch_cycle "Milankovitch cycle") lasting about 21,000 years. However, most geologists dismissed the astronomical theory. For they could not fit Milankovitch's timing to the accepted sequence, which had only four ice ages, all of them much longer than 21,000 years.[[47]](#cite_note-aipCycles-47)

In 1938 [Guy Stewart Callendar](/wiki/Guy_Stewart_Callendar "Guy Stewart Callendar") attempted to revive Arrhenius's greenhouse-effect theory. Callendar presented evidence that both temperature and the CO2 level in the atmosphere had been rising over the past half-century, and he argued that newer [spectroscopic](/wiki/Spectroscopic "Spectroscopic") measurements showed that the gas was effective in absorbing infrared in the atmosphere. Nevertheless, most scientific opinion continued to dispute or ignore the theory.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Increasing concern, 1950s–1960s

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Charles_David_Keeling_2001.jpg/250px-Charles_David_Keeling_2001.jpg)

Better [spectrography](/wiki/Spectrometer "Spectrometer") in the 1950s showed that CO2 and water vapor absorption lines did not overlap completely. Climatologists also realized that little water vapor was present in the upper atmosphere. Both developments showed that the CO2 greenhouse effect would not be overwhelmed by water vapor.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[37]](#cite_note-aiphistory-37)

In 1955 [Hans Suess](/wiki/Hans_Suess "Hans Suess")'s [carbon-14](/wiki/Carbon-14 "Carbon-14") isotope analysis showed that CO2 released from [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuels "Fossil fuels") was not immediately absorbed by the ocean. In 1957, better understanding of [ocean chemistry](/wiki/Ocean_chemistry "Ocean chemistry") led [Roger Revelle](/wiki/Roger_Revelle "Roger Revelle") to a realization that the ocean surface layer had limited ability to absorb carbon dioxide, also predicting the rise in levels of CO2 and later being proven by [Charles David Keeling](/wiki/Charles_David_Keeling "Charles David Keeling").[[50]](#cite_note-50) By the late 1950s, more scientists were arguing that carbon dioxide emissions could be a problem, with some projecting in 1959 that CO2 would rise 25% by the year 2000, with potentially "radical" effects on climate.[[37]](#cite_note-aiphistory-37) In the centennial of the American oil industry in 1959, organized by the American Petroleum Institute and the Columbia Graduate School of Business, [Edward Teller](/wiki/Edward_Teller "Edward Teller") said "It has been calculated that a temperature rise corresponding to a 10 per cent increase in carbon dioxide will be sufficient to melt the icecap and submerge New York. ... At present the carbon dioxide in the atmosphere has risen by 2 per cent over normal. By 1970, it will be perhaps 4 per cent, by 1980, 8 per cent, by 1990, 16 per cent if we keep on with our exponential rise in the use of purely conventional fuels."[[51]](#cite_note-51) In 1960 [Charles David Keeling](/wiki/Charles_David_Keeling "Charles David Keeling") demonstrated that the level of CO2 in the atmosphere was in fact rising. Concern mounted year by year along with the rise of the "[Keeling Curve](/wiki/Keeling_Curve "Keeling Curve")" of atmospheric CO2.

Another clue to the nature of climate change came in the mid-1960s from analysis of deep-sea cores by [Cesare Emiliani](/wiki/Cesare_Emiliani "Cesare Emiliani") and analysis of ancient corals by [Wallace Broecker](/wiki/Wallace_Broecker "Wallace Broecker") and collaborators. Rather than four long [ice ages](/wiki/Ice_ages "Ice ages"), they found a large number of shorter ones in a regular sequence. It appeared that the timing of ice ages was set by the small orbital shifts of the [Milankovitch cycles](/

enriched_points.expanded_content: Attribution science emerged in the late 1990s, utilizing climate models and statistical methods to correlate extreme weather events with anthropogenic climate change, paving the way for rigorous analysis.

Scientists now employ probabilistic models, quantifying the likelihood that climate change intensified specific extreme weather events, allowing precise assessments of human influence on climate variability.

Initiatives like World Weather Attribution conduct real-time analyses post-disaster, providing timely insights into climate change's impact on extreme weather, facilitating swift governmental and community responses.
